payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is unmarried and retired, has completed higher secondary education. He is fluent in Kashmiri and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a passion for visual arts, digital storytelling, and community heritage activities.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]

MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Aamir Khan]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0315-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|aamir.khan@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Doctor sahab, I have been having persistent cough for the past three weeks, along with fever in the evenings and some weight loss. I also feel tired all the time.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Aamir, I understand your concern. These symptoms could indicate tuberculosis. Have you been exposed to anyone with TB recently? Do you have any other medical conditions like diabetes?
Patient: No, I don't have diabetes. But I do have some breathing difficulties when I climb stairs. My father had TB when I was young, but he was treated successfully.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Given your family history and symptoms, we need to conduct some tests. Please visit SMHS Hospital tomorrow for a sputum test and chest X-ray. I will also prescribe some initial medications while we wait for the results.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. What medications should I start with?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: I'm prescribing Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol for four months. Please take them daily as prescribed. Also, maintain good nutrition and rest. Avoid smoking and alcohol during treatment.
Patient: I don't smoke or drink, but I'm worried about the cost of treatment.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Don't worry about the cost. The government provides free TB treatment at SMHS Hospital. You just need to bring your Aadhaar card and any previous medical records. We also have a patient support system to help with transportation if needed.
Patient: That's a relief. When should I come for the tests?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: Please come tomorrow between 9 AM to 11 AM. Take your [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]] with you for registration. If you have any questions before your appointment, you can call me at the hospital or email me at [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|aamir.khan@example.com]].
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will be there tomorrow.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]: You're welcome, Aamir. Remember, TB is completely curable with proper treatment. Take care and get well soon.

payload.arts_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, an enthusiastic creator of abstract folk motifs on fabric and paper, spends evenings immersed in the ragas of Pandit Bhimsen Joshi and the tabla rhythms of Ustad Zakir Hussain, while practicing lively Lavani steps during village celebrations, and draws inspiration from the vivid colors of local Kalamkari artisans.

payload.persona: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old mono‑line operator who weaves technical precision with vibrant folk art, thrives on community rituals yet juggles anxiety‑driven moods, lives on disciplined savings but occasionally splurges on colorful craft supplies; they balance imagination with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Lalita Tirvadekar comes from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu community that settled in the rural hinterland of Jalgaon, Maharashtra. Her family maintains a blend of coastal Konkani customs—such as devotion to Ganesh and sharing of coconut‑based festive foods—and the agrarian traditions of interior Maharashtra, like local fairs (shashti), collective farming rituals and village temple gatherings. This cultural mix shapes her worldview, fostering a respect for community harmony and a love for folk arts.

- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Saharsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

- age: 23

- occupation: Supervisor, Jewellery, Engraving and Precision Metal Workers
- education_level: Illiterate

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]

- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

Growing up in a joint‑family setting, she learned the importance of mutual support, hospitality and communal gatherings.

Her keen eye for market trends helps her adapt designs to customer preferences in the local market.

- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

payload.judge_reasoning: Kashmiri Arabic-script prose, all TYPEs in allow-list, 19M viral fever/URI fits persona, Latin terms/IDs expected and unproblematic.

payload.professional_persona: Zainab Khatun is an experienced community caretaker who manages a large rural household, prepares traditional local cuisine, weaves shawls, and mediates neighbourhood disputes, and they apply their practical mindset, compassionate nature and balanced curiosity to innovate within folk traditions.
payload.linguistic_persona: Zainab Khatun communicates fluently in the regional language of their village, can converse in the local Hindi dialect, and memorises Arabic verses for prayers, though they cannot read or write in any script.
payload.sports_persona: Zainab Khatun enjoys watching Jamshedpur FC football matches on TV, attends local kabaddi tournaments, and maintains personal fitness through daily morning walks and gentle yoga, and they occasionally join a senior women's walking club that meets at the village school ground.
payload.arts_persona: Zainab Khatun immerses themselves in the regional folk song repertoire, especially the soulful renditions of the late Lalu Mishra, relishes the poetry of local bard Bhola Bhiya, and delights in classic Hindi cinema songs of Lata Mangeshkar and Kishore Kumar, and they often hum these tunes while weaving shawls.
payload.travel_persona: Zainab Khatun prefers short, purpose-driven trips such as travelling to the nearest town market in Hazaribagh for fresh produce, visiting relatives in Ranchi during festive seasons, and occasionally joining organized pilgrim tours to the Shravan Temple in Deoghar, and they balance modest travel budgets with a love for family reunions.
payload.culinary_persona: Zainab Khatun excels at preparing traditional local dishes such as mutton keema, spicy dal with mustard seed, sewai sweet flavored with cardamom and saffron, and seasonal vegetable bhaji from their kitchen garden, preferring fresh, locally sourced ingredients while occasionally experimenting with a pinch of modern spices, and they take great pride in serving these meals to family and guests.
payload.persona: Zainab Khatun combines a lifelong devotion to community caretaking with a pragmatic curiosity, weaving stories and shawls while juggling budgeting, health rituals, and occasional sweet indulgences, and they are guided by a steady, compassionate temperament.
payload.cultural_background: Zainab Khatun belongs to a Maithili‑speaking Muslim community that has lived for generations in the rural hinterland of Hazaribagh, Jharkhand. The community blends the linguistic and folkloric traditions of the Maithili culture—such as panche dhun folk songs, seasonal fairs, and oral storytelling—with Islamic religious practices like daily prayers, Ramadan fasting, and Eid celebrations. Village life is organised around joint families, agricultural cycles, and a strong sense of neighbourly reciprocity, which have shaped Zainab’s worldview and daily routine.
payload.linguistic_background: Zainab grew up speaking Maithili as her mother tongue and uses it as her primary mode of communication at home and in the village. She can understand and converse in spoken Hindi, especially the local dialect, but due to her lack of formal schooling she cannot read or write in any script. For religious purposes she has learned to recite Arabic verses from memory and can follow the Urdu script of the Qur'an with the help of family members. Her oral proficiency enables her to preserve and transmit folk stories and culinary knowledge across generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Zainab’s expertise lies in managing a large rural household, preparing traditional Maithili Muslim cuisine, preserving oral folklore, providing informal caregiving and community support, performing basic arithmetic for budgeting, weaving handicrafts, and mediating disputes within the neighbourhood. Her practical mindset and compassionate nature allow her to apply these skills with reliability and flexibility.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Maithili Muslim cooking", "Oral folklore preservation", "Community caregiving", "Basic arithmetic budgeting", "Handicraft weaving", "Neighbourhood dispute mediation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Zainab enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, weaving colorful shawls, tending a small kitchen garden of seasonal vegetables, reciting du’a and Qur’anic verses from memory, sharing stories with grandchildren, and participating in women’s self‑help groups that discuss health, nutrition, and religious topics.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk music", "Weaving colorful shawls", "Tending a kitchen garden", "Reciting du'a and Qur'anic verses from memory", "Storytelling with grandchildren", "Participating in women’s self‑help groups"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 65, Zainab aspires to remain an active pillar of her community. She wishes to mentor younger women in traditional cooking and weaving, document oral stories for future generations, and organize health and hygiene awareness camps for the villagers. She also hopes to support her grandchildren’s education through informal tutoring and to continue her voluntary work at the mosque, ensuring that the values of compassion and harmony are passed on.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand, Hazaribagh, who is widowed and retired, speaks Maithili at home and has limited formal education. She lives in a rural area and manages household responsibilities while maintaining a traditional lifestyle. Her daily routine includes preparing local cuisine and engaging in community activities.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.

She lives in a rural area and manages household responsibilities while maintaining a traditional lifestyle. Her daily routine includes preparing local cuisine and engaging in community activities.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Hazaribagh]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-JH-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Devi]] | DOB [[DOB|1959-04-12]] | Age: [[AGE|65]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]

Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4521]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Hazaribagh]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 months with weight loss and fatigue. Patient reports difficulty breathing while climbing stairs. History of hypertension for 5 years.

History of Present Illness: Patient is a 65-year-old widowed female from rural Hazaribagh presenting with dry cough for 3 months, progressively worsening. Associated with low-grade fever in evenings, night sweats, and loss of 5 kg weight. She has been taking antihypertensive medications irregularly. No known contact with TB patients.

Examination: General condition: Thin, cachectic female. Vitals: BP 140/90 mmHg, Pulse 98/min, Temp 99.2°F, RR 22/min, SpO2 92% on room air. Chest: Decreased breath sounds in right upper zone, occasional rhonchi. Cardiovascular: Regular heart sounds, no murmurs.

Investigations: Sputum for AFB - pending. Chest X-ray - suggestive of right upper lobe infiltrates. Hb: 9.8 g/dL, ESR: 85 mm/hr. Random blood sugar: 145 mg/dL.

Diagnosis: 1. Pulmonary Tuberculosis 2. Essential Hypertension 3. Iron deficiency anemia

Treatment Plan: 1. Start DOTS treatment for TB as per RNTCP protocol 2. Continue antihypertensive medications regularly 3. Iron supplements for anemia 4. Nutritional support and counseling 5. Follow-up in 2 weeks for sputum review and medication compliance check.

Counseling: Patient counseled about importance of completing full course of TB treatment, regular medication for hypertension, and dietary modifications. Relative instructed to ensure treatment compliance and accompany patient for follow-up visits.

payload.professional_persona: Zainab Khatun is an experienced community caretaker who manages a large rural household, prepares traditional local cuisine, weaves shawls, and mediates neighbourhood disputes, and they apply their practical mindset, compassionate nature and balanced curiosity to innovate within folk traditions.
payload.linguistic_persona: Zainab Khatun communicates fluently in the regional language of their village, can converse in the local Hindi dialect, and memorises Arabic verses for prayers, though they cannot read or write in any script.
payload.sports_persona: Zainab Khatun enjoys watching Jamshedpur FC football matches on TV, attends local kabaddi tournaments, and maintains personal fitness through daily morning walks and gentle yoga, and they occasionally join a senior women's walking club that meets at the village school ground.
payload.arts_persona: Zainab Khatun immerses themselves in the regional folk song repertoire, especially the soulful renditions of the late Lalu Mishra, relishes the poetry of local bard Bhola Bhiya, and delights in classic Hindi cinema songs of Lata Mangeshkar and Kishore Kumar, and they often hum these tunes while weaving shawls.
payload.travel_persona: Zainab Khatun prefers short, purpose-driven trips such as travelling to the nearest town market in Hazaribagh for fresh produce, visiting relatives in Ranchi during festive seasons, and occasionally joining organized pilgrim tours to the Shravan Temple in Deoghar, and they balance modest travel budgets with a love for family reunions.
payload.culinary_persona: Zainab Khatun excels at preparing traditional local dishes such as mutton keema, spicy dal with mustard seed, sewai sweet flavored with cardamom and saffron, and seasonal vegetable bhaji from their kitchen garden, preferring fresh, locally sourced ingredients while occasionally experimenting with a pinch of modern spices, and they take great pride in serving these meals to family and guests.
payload.persona: Zainab Khatun combines a lifelong devotion to community caretaking with a pragmatic curiosity, weaving stories and shawls while juggling budgeting, health rituals, and occasional sweet indulgences, and they are guided by a steady, compassionate temperament.
payload.cultural_background: Zainab Khatun belongs to a Maithili‑speaking Muslim community that has lived for generations in the rural hinterland of Hazaribagh, Jharkhand. The community blends the linguistic and folkloric traditions of the Maithili culture—such as panche dhun folk songs, seasonal fairs, and oral storytelling—with Islamic religious practices like daily prayers, Ramadan fasting, and Eid celebrations. Village life is organised around joint families, agricultural cycles, and a strong sense of neighbourly reciprocity, which have shaped Zainab’s worldview and daily routine.
payload.linguistic_background: Zainab grew up speaking Maithili as her mother tongue and uses it as her primary mode of communication at home and in the village. She can understand and converse in spoken Hindi, especially the local dialect, but due to her lack of formal schooling she cannot read or write in any script. For religious purposes she has learned to recite Arabic verses from memory and can follow the Urdu script of the Qur'an with the help of family members. Her oral proficiency enables her to preserve and transmit folk stories and culinary knowledge across generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Zainab’s expertise lies in managing a large rural household, preparing traditional Maithili Muslim cuisine, preserving oral folklore, providing informal caregiving and community support, performing basic arithmetic for budgeting, weaving handicrafts, and mediating disputes within the neighbourhood. Her practical mindset and compassionate nature allow her to apply these skills with reliability and flexibility.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Maithili Muslim cooking", "Oral folklore preservation", "Community caregiving", "Basic arithmetic budgeting", "Handicraft weaving", "Neighbourhood dispute mediation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Zainab enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, weaving colorful shawls, tending a small kitchen garden of seasonal vegetables, reciting du’a and Qur’anic verses from memory, sharing stories with grandchildren, and participating in women’s self‑help groups that discuss health, nutrition, and religious topics.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk music", "Weaving colorful shawls", "Tending a kitchen garden", "Reciting du'a and Qur'anic verses from memory", "Storytelling with grandchildren", "Participating in women’s self‑help groups"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 65, Zainab aspires to remain an active pillar of her community. She wishes to mentor younger women in traditional cooking and weaving, document oral stories for future generations, and organize health and hygiene awareness camps for the villagers. She also hopes to support her grandchildren’s education through informal tutoring and to continue her voluntary work at the mosque, ensuring that the values of compassion and harmony are passed on.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand's Hazaribagh district, Maithili-speaking and widowed, with no formal education, resides in a rural setting. She is retired and manages household responsibilities, with limited English proficiency. Her lifestyle includes traditional cooking, daily walks, and community engagement.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Hazaribagh.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 65
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Hazaribagh
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand's Hazaribagh district, Maithili-speaking and widowed, with no formal education, resides in a rural setting. She is retired and manages household responsibilities, with limited English proficiency. Her lifestyle includes traditional cooking, daily walks, and community engagement.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=32. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thiruvananthapuram.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Labourer, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Thiruvananthapuram
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: Megha John is a 32-year-old female labourer from a rural area in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, who is married and holds a graduate degree. She speaks Malayalam and functional English, and works in construction and agriculture. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional yoga, with no significant sports or travel history.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- സെഷൻ ---
രോഗി [[PATIENT_NAME|Megha John]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]]
അപ്പോയിന്റ്‌മെന്റ് [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0321-001]] പോർട്ടൽ [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
ക്ലയന്റ് IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] ഡിവൈസ് IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] ഫോൺ [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
ഇമെയിൽ [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|megha.john@example.com]] ആശുപത്രി [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital, Thiruvananthapuram]]
--- ചാറ്റ് ---
രോഗി: നമസ്കാരം ഡോക്ടർ, ഞാൻ മേഘ ജോൺ, 32 വയസ്സുള്ള, 28 ആഴ്ച ഗർഭിണിയാണ്. ഇന്നലെ മുതൽ എന്റെ പാദങ്ങളിലും കൈകളിലും ചില വീക്കങ്ങൾ ഉണ്ടാകുന്നുണ്ട്. കൂടാതെ വളരെ ക്ഷീണം തോന്നുകയും ചെറിയ തലവേദന ഉണ്ടാവുകയും ചെയ്യുന്നു. ദയവായി ഉപദേശിക്കാമോ?

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: നമസ്കാരം മേഘ. നിങ്ങളുടെ ആശങ്ക എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു. 28 ആഴ്ചകളിൽ പാദങ്ങളിലും കൈകളിലും വീക്കം ഉണ്ടാകുന്നത് സാധാരണമാണ്, എങ്കിലും നമ്മൾ അത് ശ്രദ്ധാപൂർവ്വം നിരീക്ഷിക്കേണ്ടതുണ്ട്. നിങ്ങളുടെ വീട്ടിൽ ഒരു മെഷീൻ ഉണ്ടെങ്കിൽ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം പരിശോധിക്കുക. തലവേദനയ്ക്ക്, ഇരുണ്ട മുറിയിൽ വിശ്രമിക്കാനും ധാരാളം വെള്ളം കുടിക്കാനും ശ്രമിക്കുക. ദീർഘനേരം നിൽക്കുന്നത് ഒഴിവാക്കുക.

രോഗി: എന്റെ വീട്ടിൽ ബിപി മെഷീൻ ഇല്ല ഡോക്ടർ. എന്റെ ഭർത്താവ് നിർമ്മാണ മേഖലയിൽ ജോലി ചെയ്യുന്നു, ഞങ്ങൾ ഒരു ഗ്രാമപ്രദേശത്താണ് താമസിക്കുന്നത്. അധിക പരിശോധനകൾക്ക് ഞങ്ങൾക്ക് പണമില്ല.

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ സാഹചര്യം എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു മേഘ. പരിശോധനയ്ക്കായി നാളെ രാവിലെ ആശുപത്രിയിൽ വരിക. ആശുപത്രി സൗജന്യ ബിപി പരിശോധനയും അടിസ്ഥാന പരിശോധനകളും നൽകും. കഠിനമായ വീക്കം, പെട്ടെന്നുള്ള ഭാരക്കൂടുതൽ, അല്ലെങ്കിൽ കാഴ്ചയിൽ മാറ്റങ്ങൾ എന്നിവ ശ്രദ്ധയിൽപ്പെട്ടാൽ ഉടൻ വരികയോ എമർജൻസി വിളിക്കുകയോ ചെയ്യുക.

രോഗി: നന്ദി ഡോക്ടർ. എന്റെ ഭക്ഷണത്തെക്കുറിച്ച് എന്ത്? ഞാൻ ചോറും മീൻ കറിയും പച്ചക്കറികളും കഴിക്കുന്നുണ്ട്. അത് മതിയോ?

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ ഭക്ഷണരീതി നല്ലതാണ് മേഘ. മീൻ, മുട്ട, ഇലക്കറികൾ എന്നിവ കഴിക്കുന്നത് തുടരുക. പപ്പായ, വാഴപ്പഴം തുടങ്ങിയ കൂടുതൽ പഴങ്ങൾ ചേർക്കുക. ദിവസവും കുറഞ്ഞത് 8-10 ഗ്ലാസ് വെള്ളം കുടിക്കുക. സംസ്കരിച്ച ഭക്ഷണങ്ങളും അമിതമായ ഉപ്പും ഒഴിവാക്കുക. നിങ്ങളുടെ ഇരുമ്പ്, കാൽസ്യം ഗുളികകൾ കൃത്യമായി കഴിക്കുക.

രോഗി: കഴിഞ്ഞ എഎൻസി (ANC) സന്ദർശനത്തിൽ നൽകിയ ഗുളികകൾ ഞാൻ കഴിക്കുന്നുണ്ട്. അടുത്ത അപ്പോയിന്റ്‌മെന്റിനായി ഞാൻ എപ്പോൾ വരണം?

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: നിങ്ങളുടെ അടുത്ത അപ്പോയിന്റ്‌മെന്റ് 32 ആഴ്ചകളിലേക്ക് നിശ്ചയിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ട്, അതായത് 4 ആഴ്ചയ്ക്കുള്ളിൽ. ചൊവ്വാഴ്ച രാവിലെ ആശുപത്രിയിലെ ഒപിഡിയിൽ (OPD) വരിക. അതിന് മുമ്പ് എന്തെങ്കിലും ആശങ്കയുണ്ടെങ്കിൽ പോർട്ടൽ വഴി എനിക്ക് സന്ദേശം അയക്കാം അല്ലെങ്കിൽ ആശുപത്രിയിലെ റിസപ്ഷനിൽ വിളിക്കാം.

രോഗി: വളരെ നന്ദി ഡോക്ടർ. നിങ്ങൾ വളരെ സഹായിച്ചു.

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: സ്വാഗതം മേഘ. ശ്രദ്ധിക്കുക, വിശ്രമിക്കുക. 4 ആഴ്ചയ്ക്കുള്ളിൽ കാണാം.

--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Megha John]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0321-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|megha.john@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital, Thiruvananthapuram]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaskaram Doctor, I'm Megha John, 32 years old, 28 weeks pregnant. I'm having some swelling in my feet and hands since yesterday. Also feeling very tired and having mild headaches. Can you please advise?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Namaskaram Megha. I understand your concern. Swelling in feet and hands at 28 weeks is common but we need to monitor it carefully. Please check your blood pressure at home if you have a machine. For the headaches, try to rest in a dark room and drink plenty of water. Avoid standing for long periods.

Patient: I don't have a BP machine at home Doctor. My husband works in construction and we're from a rural area. We don't have much money for extra tests.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: I understand your situation Megha. Please come to the hospital tomorrow morning for a check-up. The hospital will provide free BP monitoring and basic tests. If you notice severe swelling, sudden weight gain, or vision changes, please come immediately or call emergency.

Patient: Thank you Doctor. What about my diet? I'm eating rice, fish curry, and vegetables. Is that enough?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Your diet sounds good Megha. Continue eating fish, eggs, and leafy vegetables. Add more fruits like papaya and banana. Drink at least 8-10 glasses of water daily. Avoid processed foods and excess salt. Take your iron and calcium tablets regularly.

Patient: I'm taking the tablets given at the last ANC visit. When should I come for the next appointment?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Your next appointment is scheduled for 32 weeks, which is in 4 weeks. Please come to the hospital OPD on Tuesday morning. If you have any concerns before then, you can message me through the portal or call the hospital reception.

Patient: Thank you so much Doctor. You've been very helpful.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: You're welcome Megha. Take care and take rest. We'll see you in 4 weeks.
