payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Ganjam.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Telugu for Telugu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Cleaner, Window
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Odisha
- district: Ganjam
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Bibhuti Raut, 21, is a male, never married, primary-educated window cleaner working in the urban Ganjam district of Odisha. He speaks Telugu as his first language and functional Odia for daily use. His lifestyle includes modest frugality and a routine of physical activity.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
డిశ్చార్జ్ సమ్మరీ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College & Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Bibhuti Raut]] పుట్టిన తేదీ [[DOB|2003-08-15]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Male]]
చేర్చుకున్న తేదీ [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0712]] వార్డు [[WARD_NUMBER|A3]] బెడ్ [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
డాక్టర్ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Kumar]] | చికిత్స / సలహా: రోగికి మూడు వారాల నుండి నిరంతర దగ్గు, స్వల్ప జ్వరం మరియు బరువు తగ్గడం వంటి లక్షణాలు ఉన్నాయి. కఫ పరీక్ష ద్వారా ఊపిరితిత్తుల క్షయ అని నిర్ధారించబడింది. ఐసోనియాజిడ్, రిఫాంపిసిన్, పైరాజినామైడ్ మరియు ఇథాంబ్యుటాల్ మందులతో DOTS చికిత్సను ప్రారంభించారు. తీవ్రమైన దశలో 2 నెలల తర్వాత, రోగి లక్షణాలలో గణనీయమైన మెరుగుదల కనిపించింది. రక్తపోటు క్రమం తప్పకుండా 120/80 mmHg వద్ద పర్యవేక్షించబడింది. పూర్తి 6 నెలల కోర్సును పూర్తి చేయాలని సలహా ఇవ్వబడింది. మందులను క్రమం తప్పకుండా వాడటం మరియు తదుపరి పరీక్షల షెడ్యూల్ గురించి రోగికి అవగాహన కల్పించబడింది.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Raut]]
[[DISTRICT|Ganjam]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9437012345]]
[[ABHA_ADDRESS|bibhuti.raut@abdm]]
[[CASTE|SC]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College & Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Bibhuti Raut]] DOB [[DOB|2003-08-15]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0712]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A3]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sanjay Kumar]] | Course / advice: Patient presented with persistent cough for 3 weeks, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Sputum examination confirmed pulmonary tuberculosis. Started on DOTS therapy with Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol. After 2 months of intensive phase, patient showed significant improvement in symptoms. Blood pressure monitored regularly at 120/80 mmHg. Advised to complete full 6-month course. Patient educated on medication adherence and follow-up schedule.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Raut]]
[[DISTRICT|Ganjam]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9437012345]]
[[ABHA_ADDRESS|bibhuti.raut@abdm]]
[[CASTE|SC]]
payload.judge_reasoning: Telugu prose correct; all TYPEs allowed-list; male/21/TB+BP content and Odisha/Ganjam hospital fit; Latin IDs in tags expected; no drift or collapse.

payload.professional_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a retired community culinary coordinator and church volunteer, they blend their mastery of traditional Kerala cuisine, simple bookkeeping, basic first‑aid, and informal counseling with a gentle, structured yet flexible approach that values harmony and prefers intimate gatherings over large events.
payload.linguistic_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, fluent in Malayalam and able to read and write it comfortably, they manage household accounts and correspond with relatives in Malayalam, while their English reading is limited to church newsletters and simple recipes, and they have no knowledge of Hindi or any other languages.
payload.sports_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, enjoys casual cricket matches with neighborhood friends and follows the Kerala Blasters in the Indian Super League, they participate in a local senior walking club that meets twice a week along the Thrissur Riverfront, preferring low‑impact activities over intense workouts.
payload.arts_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a lover of Malayalam devotional music by K. J. Yesudas and classic cinema featuring Mohanlal and Adoor Gopalakrishnan, they regularly attend church choir rehearsals, enjoy reading O. N. V. Kurup’s poetry, and find comfort in watching timeless Malayalam films such as 'Nirmalyam' and 'Chemmeen'.
payload.travel_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, prefers low‑key, family‑oriented trips over grand tours, they enjoy short getaways to Kerala’s backwaters, Munnar’s tea estates, and Cochin’s historic Fort, while dreaming of a modest pilgrimage to Sri Lanka’s ancient temples, planning each journey with a simple itinerary and a modest budget.
payload.culinary_persona: Sophia Devassykutty, a master of traditional Kerala fare, they expertly prepare Sadhya with banana leaf presentation, aromatic fish curry using tamarind and coconut oil, fluffy appam with coconut milk, and spicy puttu with kadala, relishing the fragrant notes of curry leaves, mustard seeds, and fresh banana saplings from their balcony garden.
payload.persona: Sophia Devassykutty blends disciplined culinary tradition with a relaxed, slightly disorganized lifestyle, cherishing family, modest budgeting, and quiet devotion to service, while occasionally postponing bills and obsessively knitting scarves for loved ones.
payload.cultural_background: Sophia belongs to the Syrian Christian community of Thrissur, Kerala, a city known for its vibrant festivals and strong communal ties. Growing up in an urban middle‑class household, she was immersed in the unique blend of Kerala’s Malayali culture—its cuisine, music, and temple festivals—while practicing Christian traditions such as Christmas, Easter, and weekly Mass. The close‑knit neighbourhoods of Thrissur, with their emphasis on family, hospitality and community service, have shaped her outlook and daily routines.
payload.linguistic_background: Sophia's mother tongue is Malayalam, which she reads, writes and speaks fluently. Her primary education gave her functional literacy in Malayalam, allowing her to manage household accounts, read religious texts, and correspond with relatives through letters. She has limited English reading ability, primarily for church newsletters and simple recipes, but does not use English in daily conversation. She does not speak Hindi or any other language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sophia is highly skilled in traditional Kerala cuisine, especially preparing Sadhya, fish curries, appam and puttu. She manages household finances, keeping simple ledgers for monthly expenses. Her practical expertise includes basic first‑aid, home gardening of spices and banana plants, and embroidery/knitting for family gifts. She also coordinates church events, arranging meals and volunteers, and offers informal counseling to younger women in her community.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Kerala cooking (Sadhya, fish curry, appam, puttu)', 'Household financial management', 'Basic first aid', 'Home gardening (spices, banana plants)', 'Embroidery and knitting', 'Church event coordination', 'Informal counseling for community women']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Sophia enjoys cooking traditional Kerala dishes, especially for family gatherings and church feasts. She loves tending to her small balcony garden where she grows curry leaves, basil, and banana saplings. In her leisure time, she listens to Malayalam devotional music, watches classic Malayalam movies, reads light Malayalam poetry, and participates in the church choir on special occasions. She also relaxes by knitting scarves for relatives and taking gentle walks along the riverfront.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Cooking traditional Kerala dishes', 'Balcony gardening', 'Listening to Malayalam devotional music', 'Watching classic Malayalam movies', 'Reading Malayalam poetry', 'Participating in church choir', 'Knitting scarves', 'Walking along the riverfront']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Sophia aims to preserve her family’s culinary heritage by documenting recipes in a handwritten cookbook to pass on to her grandchildren. She wishes to expand her role in the church by leading the women’s fellowship group, organizing health‑awareness sessions, and mentoring younger mothers. Additionally, she hopes to maintain good health through daily walks and a small vegetable garden, ensuring she remains an active, supportive presence in her family and community.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old female from Thrissur, Kerala, who is currently married and retired, completed primary education and speaks Malayalam. She resides in an urban zone and identifies as part of the Syrian Christian community. Her lifestyle includes modest budgeting, regular church attendance, and a disciplined yet relaxed approach to household management.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thrissur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Thrissur, Kerala, who is currently married and retired, completed primary education and speaks Malayalam. She resides in an urban zone and identifies as part of the Syrian Christian community. Her lifestyle includes modest budgeting, regular church attendance, and a disciplined yet relaxed approach to household management.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

രോഗി [[PATIENT_NAME|നിർമല ജോഷി]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
അപ്പോയിന്റ്മെന്റ് [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] പോർട്ടൽ [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
ക്ലയന്റ് ഐപി [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] ഡിവൈസ് ഐഎംഇഐ [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

ഇമെയിൽ [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|nirmala.joshi@example.com]] ആശുപത്രി [[HOSPITAL_NAME|മെഡിക്കൽ കോളേജ് ആശുപത്രി തിരുശൂർ]]
--- ചാറ്റ് ---
രോഗി: സുപ്രഭാതം ഡോക്ടർ. കഴിഞ്ഞ രണ്ട് ദിവസമായി എന്റെ നെഞ്ചിൽ ഒരു അസ്വസ്ഥതയുണ്ട്. അത് ഒരു മന്ദമായ വേദനയാണ്, അല്ലാതെ കഠിനമായ വേദനയല്ല. ചിലപ്പോൾ അടുത്തുള്ള ക്ഷേത്രത്തിലേക്ക് നടക്കുമ്പോൾ നെഞ്ചിൽ ഒരു സമ്മർദ്ദം അനുഭവപ്പെടാറുണ്ട്.

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|ഡോ. രവി]]: സുപ്രഭാതം ഡോ. രവി. നെഞ്ചിലെ അസ്വസ്ഥതയെക്കുറിച്ചുള്ള നിങ്ങളുടെ ആശങ്ക എനിക്ക് മനസ്സിലാകുന്നു. ഇത് എപ്പോൾ തുടങ്ങി എന്നും ഇതിന് മുമ്പ് സമാനമായ അനുഭവങ്ങൾ ഉണ്ടായിട്ടുണ്ടോ എന്നും കൂടുതൽ പറയാമോ?

രോഗി: എന്റെ പ്രഭാത നടത്തത്തിന് തൊട്ടുപിന്നാലെ രണ്ട് ദിവസം മുമ്പ് ഇത് തുടങ്ങി. എനിക്ക് ഇതിനുമുമ്പ് ഇത് ഉണ്ടായിട്ടില്ല. എനിക്ക് 56 വയസ്സുണ്ട്, വിരമിച്ച ഗൃഹിനിയാണ്. എന്റെ ഭർത്താവ് എന്റെ കൂടെയുണ്ട്. പനിയില്ല, ശ്വാസംമുട്ടലുമില്ല.

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|ഡോ. രവി]]: വിശദാംശങ്ങൾക്ക് നന്ദി. നിങ്ങളുടെ പ്രായവും ലക്ഷണങ്ങളും കണക്കിലെടുത്ത്, ഒരു ഇസിജിക്കും അടിസ്ഥാന രക്തപരിശോധനകൾക്കുമായി ആശുപത്രിയിൽ വരാൻ ഞാൻ ശുപാർശ ചെയ്യുന്നു. ദയവായി നാളെ രാവിലെ മെഡിക്കൽ കോളേജ് ആശുപത്രി തിരുശൂരിലേക്ക് വരിക.

രോഗി: എനിക്ക് നാളെ വരാൻ കഴിയും. ഞാൻ ഏത് സമയത്താണ് വരേണ്ടത്? ഞാൻ എന്തെങ്കിലും കൊണ്ടുവരണോ?

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|ഡോ. രവി]]: ദയവായി രാവിലെ 9 മണിക്ക് വരിക. നിങ്ങളുടെ പഴയ മെഡിക്കൽ റെക്കോർഡുകൾ ഉണ്ടെങ്കിൽ അത് കൊണ്ടുവരിക, കൂടാതെ നിങ്ങളുടെ ആധാർ കാർഡും. ഞങ്ങൾ ഒരു ഇസിജി ചെയ്യുകയും നിങ്ങളുടെ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദവും രക്തത്തിലെ പഞ്ചസാരയുടെ അളവും പരിശോധിക്കുകയും ചെയ്യും.

രോഗി: നന്ദി ഡോക്ടർ. ഞാൻ നാളെ അവിടെ ഉണ്ടാകും. മറ്റൊരു കാര്യം - ഞാൻ ഇരിക്കുന്ന സ്ഥാനത്ത് നിന്ന് പെട്ടെന്ന് എഴുന്നേൽക്കുമ്പോൾ ചിലപ്പോൾ തലകറക്കം അനുഭവപ്പെടാറുണ്ട്.

ഡോക്ടർ [[DOCTOR_NAME|ഡോ. രവി]]: അത് ശ്രദ്ധിക്കേണ്ട കാര്യമാണ്. എഴുന്നേൽക്കുമ്പോൾ ശ്രദ്ധിക്കുക. ഞങ്ങൾ വിവിധ നിലകളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ രക്തസമ്മർദ്ദം പരിശോധിക്കും. നാളെ രാവിലെ 9 മണിക്ക് കാണാം.

--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Nirmala Joshi]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0815-001]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|mary.thomas@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Medical College Hospital Thrissur]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Good morning doctor. I'm having some discomfort in my chest area for the past two days. It's a dull ache, not sharp pain. Sometimes it feels like there's pressure when I walk to the temple nearby.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Good morning Mary. I understand your concern about the chest discomfort. Can you tell me more about when this started and if you've experienced anything similar before?

Patient: It started two days ago, just after my morning walk. I've never had this before. I'm 56 years old, retired homemaker. My husband is here with me. No fever, no breathing difficulty.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Thank you for the details. Given your age and symptoms, I'd like to recommend you visit the hospital for an ECG and basic blood tests. Please come to Medical College Hospital Thrissur tomorrow morning.

Patient: I can come tomorrow. What time should I come? Should I bring anything?

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: Please come by 9 AM. Bring your previous medical records if any, and your Aadhaar card. We'll do an ECG and check your blood pressure and blood sugar levels.

Patient: Thank you doctor. I'll be there tomorrow. One more thing - I sometimes feel dizzy when I stand up quickly from sitting.

Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Nair]]: That's important to note. Please be careful when standing up. We'll check your blood pressure in different positions. See you tomorrow at 9 AM.

Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Amravati.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Amravati
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker from Amravati, Maharashtra, with below-primary education, who speaks Urdu as her primary language. She lives in an urban setting and presents with anxiety that influences daily decision-making. Her lifestyle includes managing household budgets, preparing traditional Maharashtrian meals, and engaging in community charity work.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ریڈیولوجی رپورٹ: [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Amravati]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Patil]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MH-2024-0815-0042]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]] | رپورٹ کردہ ڈاکٹر ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
[[DISTRICT|Amravati]] | [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] | [[HOSPITAL_ID|CH-AMR-001]] | [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

نتائج:
کنٹراسٹ کے ساتھ CT پیٹ ایک 6.2 سینٹی میٹر کی غیر ہم جنس رسولی ظاہر کرتا ہے جو دائیں ایڈرینل غدود سے پیدا ہو رہی ہے، جس میں مردہ بافتوں (necrosis) اور کیلسیفیکیشن کے علاقے موجود ہیں۔ رسولی کی حدود غیر منظم ہیں اور غیر ہم جنس اضافہ (enhancement) ہے۔ ملحقہ دائیں گردے اور انفیریئر وینا کاوا میں مقامی حملے کے شواہد موجود ہیں۔ ریٹروپیریٹونیم میں کوئی نمایاں لمف ایڈینوپیتھی نہیں پائی گئی۔ جگر بغیر کسی فوکل لیژنز کے نارمل نظر آتا ہے۔ تلی اور لبلبہ غیر معمولی ہیں۔ آنتوں کے لوپس ظاہری طور پر نارمل ہیں۔

تاثر:
دائیں ایڈرینل کی رسولی جس کی خصوصیات کینسر (malignancy) کے لیے تشویشناک ہیں، غالباً مقامی حملے کے ساتھ ایڈرینل کورٹیکل کارسینوما۔ مزید انتظام کے لیے سرجیکل مشاورت کی سفارش کی جاتی ہے۔

سفارشات:
ممکنہ ایڈرینالیکٹومی کے لیے فوری سرجیکل تشخیص۔ اگر سرجیکل پلاننگ کے لیے اضافی ٹشو تشخیص کی ضرورت ہو تو آپریشن سے پہلے بایوپسی پر غور کریں۔
payload.generated_text_en:
Radiology Report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Amravati]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Patil]] [[AGE|56]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MH-2024-0815-0042]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
[[DISTRICT|Amravati]] | [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] | [[HOSPITAL_ID|CH-AMR-001]] | [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

FINDINGS:
CT abdomen with contrast demonstrates a 6.2 cm heterogeneous mass arising from the right adrenal gland with areas of necrosis and calcification. The mass shows irregular margins and heterogeneous enhancement. There is evidence of local invasion into the adjacent right kidney and inferior vena cava. No significant lymphadenopathy is identified in the retroperitoneum. Liver appears normal without focal lesions. Spleen and pancreas are unremarkable. Bowel loops are normal in appearance.

IMPRESSION:
Right adrenal mass with features concerning for malignancy, likely adrenal cortical carcinoma with local invasion. Surgical consultation recommended for further management.

RECOMMENDATION:
Urgent surgical evaluation for possible adrenalectomy. Consider preoperative biopsy if surgical planning requires additional tissue diagnosis.
payload.judge_reasoning: Urdu-script prose matches expected language; all TYPEs allow-listed; female 56yo adrenal mass fits surgical/radiology domain with no persona mismatch or script shift.

payload.professional_persona: Rajeshwari Satari, a 22‑year‑old aspiring tiffin‑service entrepreneur, blends practical cooking expertise with hands‑on cash‑book keeping, yet their relaxed approach to planning sometimes causes missed market opportunities.
payload.linguistic_persona: Rajeshwari Satari is fluent in Urdu, reading and writing it effortlessly, but they can only grasp basic spoken Kannada and recognise a few Hindi words from songs, reflecting limited formal literacy.
payload.sports_persona: Rajeshwari Satari enjoys regular walks with her dog across the farmland and occasionally joins informal village cricket matches, cheering for Royal Challengers Bangalore, though they rarely attend a formal gym.
payload.arts_persona: Rajeshwari Satari immerses herself in Urdu ghazals by Mirza Ghalib and the melodious voice of Lata Mangeshkar, knits simple yarn scarves, and hand‑weaves modest cotton fabrics, finding artistic fulfillment both in literature and textile crafts.
payload.travel_persona: Rajeshwari Satari prefers low‑cost weekend trips by state bus to nearby towns like Gulbarga or Bidar, exploring historic forts and local markets, while dreaming of an eventual pilgrimage to Hyderabad’s old city for a ghazal evening.
payload.culinary_persona: Rajeshwari Satari delights in cooking traditional Karnataka dishes such as jolada rotti with sambar, sweet holige, and spiced okra stir‑fry, favouring fresh coriander, coconut, tamarind and jaggery, though they rely mostly on family recipes rather than formal culinary training.
payload.persona: Rajeshwari Satari is a solitary Urdu‑loving aspiring tiffin‑service provider who balances practical cooking and cash‑keeping with a habit of worrying and occasional disorganized impulse buying.
payload.cultural_background: Rajeshwari grew up in a rural village near Gulbarga, Karnataka, where the cultural landscape blends Deccani Urdu traditions with the local Kannada way of life. Her family is Hindu, but Urdu is the language spoken at home, a legacy of the erstwhile Hyderabad State that once covered this region. She celebrates Hindu festivals such as Ugadi and Diwali, while also taking part in community gatherings that feature Urdu poetry recitals. The agrarian setting, joint‑family households, and emphasis on home‑made crafts shape her everyday experience.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajeshwari’s mother tongue is Urdu, and she is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing it in the Perso‑Arabic script. Because she left school before reaching primary level, her formal literacy in Kannada and Hindi is limited to basic recognition of signs and a few words she hears in the village. She can understand simple spoken Kannada used for daily transactions, but she cannot read or write it. Her exposure to Hindi is mainly through religious songs and occasional media, giving her a rudimentary listening comprehension.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajeshwari is skilled in traditional household cooking, especially preparing Karnataka staples like jolada rotti, sambar, and sweet delicacies for festivals. She can hand‑loom basic cotton fabric and weave simple designs for household use. Her fluency in Urdu allows her to read and write letters, compose poetry, and preserve family stories in the language. She manages household finances through simple cash‑book keeping, and she is proficient in caring for young children, including basic first‑aid measures. Additionally, she tends a modest kitchen garden, knowing how to cultivate seasonal vegetables.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking (Karnataka cuisine)', 'Traditional handloom weaving', 'Urdu reading & writing', 'Household cash bookkeeping', 'Basic childcare & first aid', 'Kitchen gardening']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rajeshwari finds solace in solitary activities such as listening to Urdu ghazals and reciting poetry from classical poets like Mirza Ghalib. She enjoys tending to her kitchen garden, growing seasonal vegetables like okra, beans, and leafy greens. Cooking experiments for family celebrations give her a creative outlet. She also likes knitting simple yarn scarves during quiet evenings and watching Hindi serials on the small television set. Occasionally, she walks along the fields with her dog, using the time for personal reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Urdu ghazals', 'Reciting poetry', 'Kitchen gardening', 'Experimenting with festive cooking', 'Knitting simple scarves', 'Watching Hindi TV serials', 'Walking in fields with her dog']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajeshwari wishes to improve her literacy in Urdu and acquire basic reading skills in Kannada so she can handle market transactions independently. She aspires to turn her cooking talent into a small tiffin‑service business that supplies packed meals to daily wage workers in nearby towns. Over the next few years she hopes to learn simple bookkeeping and, if possible, a tailoring skill that would let her sell embroidered household linens. Ultimately, she wants a stable source of income that reduces the family’s financial anxieties and gives her a sense of personal achievement.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language. She is a never-married homemaker who manages household finances and cooking, with limited formal employment. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a habit of worrying, while she prefers low-cost local travel and enjoys traditional cooking.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Urdu (code=ur, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
MATERNAL SAFETY: clinical content must be age-plausible for persona age=22. Routine ANC/delivery typically ages 18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC/PNC/contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy as a routine story.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Gulbarga.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Urdu.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Gulbarga
- first_language: Urdu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female from rural Gulbarga, Karnataka, with below-primary education and Urdu as her primary language. She is a never-married homemaker who manages household finances and cooking, with limited formal employment. Her lifestyle includes occasional impulsive spending and a habit of worrying, while she prefers low-cost local travel and enjoys traditional cooking.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.linguistic_persona: Sinnak Ammal speaks fluent Tamil, though they cannot read or write the script, and picks up a handful of conversational Hindi phrases while struggling with occasional English words, relying heavily on oral instructions and memory for their delivery routes.
payload.sports_persona: Sinnak Ammal is a devout fan of cricket, cheering for the Chennai Super Kings during IPL seasons and follows local badminton matches at the community centre, while they occasionally join a neighbourhood walking club for early‑morning strolls to stay active.
payload.arts_persona: Sinnak Ammal finds joy in listening to Tamil folk songs like Mennesum Poo, devotional bhajans of M.S. Subbulakshmi, and the classic film scores of Ilaiyaraaja, often humming along while cooking, and they also enjoy watching Rajinikanth movies on weekend evenings.
payload.travel_persona: Sinnak Ammal enjoys occasional weekend trips to nearby Mahabalipuram to admire stone temples, a nostalgic ferry ride to Pondicherry for its sea‑breezy lanes, and dreams of a hill‑station retreat in Kodaikanal, though family responsibilities often keep them grounded in Thiruvallur, and they can rarely travel beyond.
payload.culinary_persona: Sinnak Ammal loves cooking hearty sambar with tamarind and mustard seeds, tangy rasam flavoured with curry leaves and black pepper, and enjoys the simple comfort of idli and dosa topped with coconut chutney, and they often pause to chat with neighbours about the day’s news while sipping filter coffee.
payload.persona: A diligent newspaper courier at 40, Sinnak Ammal balances a caring nature and practical curiosity with a lingering worry about money and health, all while they dream of a tiny kiosk that blends old‑school news with modern services.
payload.cultural_background: Sinnak Ammal was born and raised in an urban neighbourhood of Thiruvallur, a rapidly growing suburb of Chennai. She belongs to a Tamil Hindu family where daily life blends age‑old customs with the bustling rhythm of a modern town. The streets around her home are lined with temples, small tea stalls and the early‑morning echo of newspaper deliveries. She has celebrated festivals such as Pongal, Aadi Perukku and the Tamil New Year with great enthusiasm, which has reinforced community spirit, hospitality and respect for nature. This cultural fabric values modesty, reverence for elders and a practical work ethic – a blend that mirrors her practical outlook and curiosity about the world around her. She is occasionally curious about the rise of digital news, yet she still values the tangible feel of a printed newspaper.
payload.linguistic_background: Sinnak Ammal's mother tongue is Tamil. Because she never attended school, she is illiterate in Tamil script, meaning she cannot read or write fluently, but she can understand spoken Tamil well and can recognize common words and numerals on newspaper addresses. She has never learned Hindi or English, but due to being in an urban Tamil Nadu setting and interacting with Hindi‑speaking customers occasionally, she picks up a few conversational phrases. Her literacy limitation requires her to rely on oral instructions and memory for her delivery route, which sometimes makes her anxious when she has to handle cash receipts or understand printed notices.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Through years of delivering newspapers in Thiruvallur’s bustling streets, Sinnak Ammal has developed strong physical stamina, an intuitive knowledge of the neighbourhood’s layout, and reliable cash handling abilities. She is adept at building quick rapport with regular customers, remembering subscription preferences, and managing early‑morning schedules despite a relaxed planning style. Her practical curiosity helps her troubleshoot delivery hiccups on the fly. Additionally, she possesses basic skills in preparing simple tea for customers during cold mornings, contributing to her cooperative nature. However, her occasional anxiety about mis‑counting cash or missing a delivery slot makes her double‑check everything.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Newspaper distribution', 'Customer interaction', 'Physical stamina', 'Cash handling', 'Neighbourhood knowledge', 'Morning tea preparation']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In her leisure time, Sinnak Ammal enjoys listening to Tamil folk songs and devotional music while doing chores, watching cricket matches on the local television, and cooking traditional Tamil dishes such as sambar and rasam for her family. She loves attending temple festivals, where she can mingle with neighbours, and she finds comfort in simple gardening, tending to a few marigold plants on her balcony. payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Sinnak Ammal aspires to build a more stable income while remaining flexible. Over the next few years she hopes to acquire enough capital to start her own small newspaper kiosk near her home, allowing her to manage subscriptions and sell additional items such as tea and snacks. She is also curious about the rise of digital news platforms and dreams of incorporating mobile recharge services at her stall. Additionally, she plans to learn basic reading and numeracy in Tamil through community adult‑education classes, which would help her handle petty cash and keep simple accounts. Ultimately, she wants to secure financial security for her husband and children and be recognized as a trusted local vendor, while managing her anxiety about money through careful budgeting.

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Thiruvallur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Tamil for Tamil.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: News Paper Boy
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Thiruvallur
- first_language: Tamil
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female newspaper courier in urban Tamil Nadu, currently married, with an illiterate education level in Thiruvallur district. Her first language is Tamil, and she manages cash and health concerns while working. She has a practical curiosity and expresses a desire for a small business kiosk.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thiruvallur Government Medical College Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Mary Thomas]], [[AGE|40]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|MRN-TVL-2024-0042]]

Date: Today
CC: Low back pain for 2 weeks.
HPI: Patient is a 40-year-old female newspaper courier from Thiruvallur. She presents with a dull, aching pain in the lower back for the past two weeks. The pain is exacerbated by carrying heavy bundles of newspapers and improves with rest. She reports no radiating pain down the legs, numbness, or weakness. She has a history of hypertension, well-controlled on Amlodipine 5mg daily. She denies any recent trauma.
Examination: On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Lumbar spine is tender to palpation over L4-L5 region. No signs of scoliosis. Straight leg raise is negative. No motor, sensory, or reflex deficits in the lower limbs.
Investigations: Lumbar spine X-ray shows mild degenerative changes at L4-L5.
Diagnosis: Mechanical low back pain, likely due to occupational strain.
Plan:
1. Prescribed Paracetamol 500mg tablet, one tablet three times a day after food for pain relief.
2. Prescribed Cyclobenzaprine 5mg tablet, one tablet at bedtime for muscle spasm.
3. Advised adequate rest, hot fomentation to the lower back twice daily.
4. Counselled on maintaining proper posture while carrying loads and requested to avoid heavy lifting.
5. Continue Amlodipine 5mg as prescribed for hypertension.
6. Follow up in one week at the outpatient department.
Patient's Concern: Patient expressed a desire to start a small tea kiosk to reduce physical strain. Advised to discuss with family.

[[DISTRICT|Thiruvallur]]

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
